text:
RPD File No. / N° de dossier de la SPR : TB0-17375
Private Proceeding / Huis clos
Reasons and Decision ? Motifs et Décision
Claimant(s)
XXXX XXXX
Demandeur(e)(s) d'asile
Date(s) of Hearing
January 22, 2014
Date(s) de l'audience
Place of Hearing
Toronto, Ontario
Lieu de l'audience
Date of Decision
February 19, 2014
Date de la décision
and reasons
et des motifs
Panel
Edward C. Robinson
Tribunal
Counsel for the Claimant(s)
Lorne Lichtenstein
Barrister and Solicitor
Conseil(s) du (de la/des) demandeur(e)(s) d'asile
Designated Representative(s)
XXXX XXXX
Représentant(e)(s) désigné(e)(s)
Counsel for the Minister
N/A
Conseil du (de la) ministre
REASONS FOR DECISION
[1] XXXX XXXX (the claimant) claims to be a citizen of Russia and requests protection under sections 96 and 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA).1 The claimant's son, XXXX XXXX, a Canadian citizen, agreed to and acted as Designated Representative for his 83 year old mother, as it appeared that she was having difficulties understanding and answering questions. Counsel agreed for the claimant's son to act as Designated Representative.
ALLEGATIONS
[2] The following is a summary of the claimant's allegations as set out in the Personal Information Forms2 (PIF).
[3] The claimant alleged that she and her deceased husband suffered various forms of mistreatment because of their Jewish background. The claimant alleged that anti-Semitism has always been strong in Russia but in recent years it has increased greatly. She alleged that in the fall of 2008 she and her late husband began receiving threatening letters and telephone calls informing them that "Russia is for Russians." In the summer of 2009 graffiti of swastikas appeared on the doors of their apartment. They reported the incident to the police but the police considered it a prank and did nothing.
[4] On XXXX XXXX, 2010 the claimant and her husband went shopping. They were confronted by a group of Skinheads who yelled anti-Semitic threats to them. One of them pushed her husband. He fell to the ground and hit his head while he was kicked and spat on. The claimant was also assaulted. Her husband was taken to the hospital and he died three days after. The incident was reported to the police. On XXXX XXXX, 2010 the police informed the claimant that their investigation did not find any criminal basis.
[5] The claimant alleged that on XXXX XXXX, 2010 she was attacked by a Russian man and hit on the forehead and she fell. The attacker ran away and she was taken to the hospital. She reported the incident to the police but he was indifferent and hostile towards her and didn't take any action. The claimant also alleged that she continued to receive threats. Her son who lives in Canada sent an invitation letter inviting her to come to Canada for a visit. She accepted and arrived in Canada on XXXX XXXX XXXX 2010.
[6] The claimant alleged that once she arrived in Canada and her son saw her condition he asked what had happened and she told him everything. He then advised her to file for refugee protection which she did on December 10, 2010. The claimant fears if she returns to Russia she will suffer persecution because she is Jewish.
DETERMINATION
[7] The panel finds that the claimant is neither a Convention refugee nor a person in need of protection.
ANALYSIS
Identity
[8] The claimant's oral testimony and documentary evidence establish that she is a citizen of Russia.3
Credibility
[9] The panel is guided by the Federal Court of Appeal which has ruled that testimony given under oath is presumed to be true, unless there is a valid reason to doubt its truthfulness.4 The assessment the panel must use to test the truth of a story of a witness is that it be in harmony with the preponderance of probabilities which a practical and informed person would readily recognize as reasonable in that place and in those conditions.5 Furthermore, the panel cannot be satisfied that, "the evidence is credible or trustworthy unless satisfied that it is probably so, not just possibly so."6
[10] Some of the claimant's evidence was not reliable. For example, the claimant stated that on XXXX XXXX, 2010 she and her husband were attacked by skinheads, one of whom pushed her husband, he fell and hit his head. They were both taken to the hospital, she was checked by the doctors but they "did not find anything serious with her" but her husband didn't recover and died three days later.7 The DR, who is also the claimant's son living in Canada, was asked if he was told the circumstances surrounding his father's death in Russia and he said "No, I only got the information when I met my mother here." He was also asked if anyone had told him before how his father had died and he said no. Asked if he had enquired as to how his father had died and he said yes. Asked what he was told and he said that one of his friends told him that his father "was taken to the hospital and he was there for three days, and they spoke about his kidneys, never mentioned the injury, they wrote something about the kidneys because he was there without refrigeration." The panel finds his answer somewhat vague and incoherent. He was therefore asked to explain what he meant by "without refrigeration" and he said that his friend explained to him that his father had spots on his skin so they thought he had kidney failure.
[11] Sensing that there was some uncertainty about the cause of death, the DR was asked if he spoke to any of the doctors himself but he said no. Not understanding why he wouldn't have spoken to any of the doctors who examined and treated his father he was asked why not, he said that he never left Canada he was always here. The panel does not find that answer reasonable because it believes he could stay right here in Canada and speak to a doctor in Russia on the telephone. For example, he testified that his friend, although in Russia, had spoken to the doctor by telephone. In the view of this panel he could have done the same. Thus, the DR was asked, "As a son wouldn't you want to know how your dad died" the panel finds his answer instructive. He replied, "The fact that he was not with me, the fact that my friend told me that it was some kidney failure, I didn't know until my mother came to Canada." In other words, his friend had told him that his father had died from kidney failure which suggests that he was satisfied with what he was told and he saw no need for further enquiry. It was only after his mother came with a different story he had reason to believe something else.
[12] Thus, the panel finds that he was indeed told how his father had died, even if what his mother has since told him was very different.
[13] Also, according to him, his friend had told him that the doctor "wrote" something about his father's kidneys and this friend had told him that it was some kidney failure. All this contradicts his earlier answer to the question regarding whether anyone had told him how his father had died. For, again, he now says that a friend had told him that his father died of 'some kidney failure'.
[14] The panel therefore rejects the claimant's allegation that her husband was murdered, and finds on a balance of probabilities that she has used the fact that her husband died from natural causes, and through manipulation of those facts concocted a story that her husband was attacked, pushed, that he fell and hit his head and three days later died of his injures, which undermines her credibility.
[15] In support of the panel's findings it notes that the claimant didn't present the 'document' that the DR's friend mentioned to him, when he said that the doctor "wrote something" about kidney failure. Whatever this information was written on is not before the panel. Since the claimant's husband was in the hospital allegedly for three days before he died, the DR was asked if there is a medical report for the time he was in and was treated by the hospital. He replied "I don't know of any report." Asked if a medical report was requested and he replied that he didn't make a request and he is not sure that his mother did for she was so upset. Because there is no mention of kidney failure on the death certificate presented8 before the Board, in fact there is no cause of death on this death certificate, and the DR has stated that his friend told him that the doctor "wrote something about the kidneys" and that the doctor "thought he had kidney failure," this panel finds it reasonable to assume that the claimant's husband's death was from natural causes - kidney failure. As such, the allegation that the claimant's husband was attacked and assaulted by skinheads, leading to his death, is a fabrication to enhance her claim for refugee status.
[16] The DR was asked, apart from his mother saying that her husband was assaulted is there any independent evidence to support her allegation and he said no. This is especially significant because he testified that his mother did inform the doctor that her husband was assaulted. In fact, the claimant stated in her PIF narrative that the police were called to the hospital and they attended and she gave the police all the details but added that the police officer quickly left the hospital.9 As well, the claimant wrote that after her husband died she went to the police station and reported it and she went back to the police station on XXXX XXXX, 2010 and was told that there was an investigation conducted but they found nothing "suspicious or criminal" that led to her husband's death.10 Despite these trips to the police station and the claimant's interactions with the police, the DR testified that the police didn't give his mother "any official document or anything like that." At the same time when the DR was asked if his mother had signed the statement she gave to the police he replied yes "every document submitted to the police is signed." The panel finds that if she indeed submitted signed statements to the police on a balance of probabilities they would have at least given her a copy of her statement.
[17] Since the claimant was not given a copy of her statement or any document attesting to her complaint to the police, the DR was asked if his mother went to the prosecutor's office to complain against the police officers' conduct, and he said yes. The panel notes that there is no mention of the claimant's going to the prosecutor's office in her PIF narrative, which is very significant because it goes to the core of why she cannot obtain state protection in Russia. The DR was asked to explain the omission. He replied, "I don't know, I can't tell you but I know that she told me that she was checking it out with the prosecutor's office." Bear in mind, this is the same person who didn't know what was happening to his parents in Russia because they didn't tell him. He only knew about it when the claimant came to Canada and told him. However, he now testifies that she told him that "she was checking it out with the prosecutor's office" regarding the police conduct. The panel then pointed out to the DR his mother's statements in the PIF narrative that she didn't approach the prosecutor's office for she wrote "Even if I went with a complaint to the prosecutor's office, the rules are that they send it to the police department against which you complain"11 and asked him to explain the contradiction. He replied, "That is what I remember." The panel finds his explanation unreasonable which undermines his credibility.
[18] Furthermore, the DR testified that it is the prosecutor's office that provides oversight for the police. The panel also notes that the claimant wrote that she went to the police with regards to other incidents and the police treated her with indifference. So, he was asked if his mother had reported the police officers' alleged indifference to the prosecutor's office. This time he said he was not sure she did. The panel finds his answer that he was not sure his mother had reported the police officer's indifference to her complaint to the prosecutor's office vague and confusing.
[19] For example, the panel notes that the DR was specifically asked at the beginning of his testimony if he was aware of his mother's story and he told the panel yes. And while the panel does not expect him to 'know' everything about her case, it certainly expects him to know what, if anything, she did to seek state protection. Yet, he is not sure if his mother reported the police conduct to the prosecutor's office. The same office he said has oversight of the police. This is important information that he should know about her story, especially as he said that he was aware of her story. The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the DR is aware of the story, but because it is contrived, it is difficult to remember it all.
[20] In another instance, the panel asked the DR if the doctor had called the police when his mother and father had been taken to the hospital for treatment from their attack and physically assaults, he replied "That I don't know." Bear in mind that the claimant wrote in her PIF narrative that "I told them that my husband's condition was a result of the attack and they call a local police officer, who came to the hospital and I gave him the evidence there...".12 Since the DR is aware of his mother's story of persecution, how could he have not known if the doctor had called the police when it is written in her PIF narrative? His mother's written account was pointed out to him and he was asked to explain his answer in light of what his mother had written and his previous answer, he said "Maybe I didn't read the story very carefully." The panel finds his explanation unreasonable.
[21] The panel finds that the DR was trying to bolster his mother's allegation of persecution by filling in what he considered gaps in her story. And because he was caught in a credibility problem when he told the panel that his mother sought protection from the prosecutor's office when his mother didn't say she did in her PIF narrative, he became overly cautious - saying things like 'not sure', 'I don't know'.
[22] The claimant wrote that "Allegedly the police started looking into the incident"13 of her husband's death. Her reason for 'suspecting' that the police may have conducted an investigation into her husband's death, which occurred on August 13, but was precipitated by an alleged incident on XXXX XXXX, 2010, is because the death certificate was issued by the Registrar's office on XXXX XXXX. So, she alleged, without providing any evidence how she knew that usually the death certificate would be issued to the relatives the same day or the next day after a person died.14.
[23] So because the death certificate was not issued on the same day or the day after her husband died, she wants the panel to believe she is speculating that there was some form of an investigation. This is to suggest that she is somehow being reasonable for she is sort of acknowledging that the police may have done a little investigation as she went to the police station and insisted on an investigation. But a close look at the evidence reveals that the claimant was merely setting the state to make the point that the police told her that they "didn't find anything suspicious or criminal" about her husband's death after their investigation.15 The claimant will have the panel believe that a complaint about her husband's murder, which she insisted that the police investigated, and in a few days they came back with a result which she knew to be wrong because she was an eye-witness to his assault perpetrated by skinheads. But, as the panel notes, there is absolutely no persuasive evidence before it that the claimant and her husband were physically attacked on XXXX XXXX XXXX 2010. In fact, there is absolutely no independent evidence before this panel that the claimant and her husband experienced anything she alleged that they did in terms of persecution.
[24] In examining the evidence before it the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant's story of persecution was based on exaggerations and embellishments and the DR was central to the fraud. For example, the DR admitted that his parents had received threatening letters and telephone calls. However, when he was asked if he had any of the letters he said no. Asked why not and he said that he believe his parents just threw the letters into the garbage. Asked why his parents would throw out the threatening letters he replied "Why they should keep it, they never expected that something like that would happen, right." The panel finds his explanation unreasonable. The letters are evidence in an alleged criminal matter. The claimant may be elderly but she is very educated.16 When the panel pointed out that the letters were evidence and asked why they wouldn't have taken the letters as evidence to the police. At that point he replied "I don't know what they did with it, I just believe they threw it out."
[25] The panel finds that if indeed the claimant and her husband had received threatening letters, and had made complaints to the police as she said they did, on a balance of probabilities they would have handed over the letters to the police, especially as the claimant stated that the police "dismissed our complaint as a prank".17 The letters would likely have confirmed or at least supported the seriousness of their complaints. As such, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant made up the story that they received threatening letters and telephone calls and that in the summer of 2008 their apartment doors were written up with graffiti.
[26] To follow up on the point of the alleged police conduct and the oversight of the police, the panel notes the DR testified that "My mother told me that she tried to file a complaint but nobody took her complaint." Asked if his mother followed up with the police if indeed there was an investigation and the result, he replied that "I believe that she tried to find out with a prosecutor if there was any progress but they didn't give her any official document or anything like that." He was then specifically asked if his mother went to the prosecutor's office or not and he specifically said yes. Since this information is not written in the claimant's narrative he was asked to explain the omission he replied "I don't know, I wasn't there, I can't tell you but I know she told me that she was checking it with the prosecutor's office."
[27] The narrative instructions ask claimants to describe efforts to obtain protection from the state, or alternatively, why they did not make an effort. If the claimant approached the prosecutor's office, she should state it clearly in her PIF narrative, especially as she is represented by counsel. It is reasonable to expect this information would have been in the narrative. Furthermore, the narrative in fact states that the incidents were reported to police but there is no police report. The evidence with respect to these incidents is therefore not reliable and the panel finds that they did not occur as the claimant would have the panel believe.
[28] Based on all of the above, the panel finds the claimant has fabricated or exaggerated several of the central allegations of serious harm in an effort to advance or embellish a claim for protection in Canada. At the same time, the panel is aware that racial and other forms of discrimination have been on the increase in Russia:
Human rights organizations, media sources and the United Nations (UN) Committee for the Elimination of Racial Discrimination (CERD), report increased racial discrimination, xenophobia, and racially motivated violence against ethnic minorities in Russia. Potential targets of violence include people from the Caucasus, Central Asia, and Siberia, immigrants from Africa, and Asia, Roma, Meshketian Turks and other ethnic minorities. According to the SOVA Center for Information and Analysis, a Moscow-based non-profit organization which monitors hate crimes, anyone with "non-Slavic features" may be assaulted by racists in Russia.18
[29] As well, certain groups in Russia continue to engage in
[...] Violent Hate Crimes against Persons and Property: Chauvinist groups have stepped up their campaign, including death threats, against individuals, groups, and officials that defend the rights of religious and ethnic minorities and migrants. While the Russian police, particularly in Moscow, have offered some assistance to victims, these efforts are inconsistent and often ineffective. Local authorities often fail to investigate hate crimes against members of ethnic and religious minorities, leading to the problem of impunity for -skinhead racist groups' attacks on mainly Muslim Central Asians and Jews.19
[30] But there is also evidence of the Russian authority taking steps to address these problems including the problem of police misconduct. For example in an article of the Human Rights Watch titled Russia: A Move to Investigate Police Abuses dated April 19, 2012 speaks about "The creation of a special unit to investigate crimes by law enforcement officials."20
[31] The panel notes that if the claimant was to return to Russia and had problems with police misconduct with respect to police indifference to complaints against nationalists and skinheads, there is a body that deals specifically with these issues. For example,
[...] according to documentary evidence, there is the creation of a special unit to investigate crimes by law enforcement officials is an important step toward ending impunity and eliminating torture by law enforcement and security agencies in Russia Human Rights Watch said today. It will be critically important for the unit to have the independence, robust mandate authority, and resources it needs to do its job, Human Rights Watch said.21
[32] The panel also notes that actions are not only taken at the legislative level but at the operational level as well,
According to the chief of the Department of Internal Security of the Ministry of the Interior, 2,156 crimes committed by policemen had been solved in the first five months of 2010, which is about 6.5 per cent more than in the same period in 2009.22
[33] As well,
The 2010 report by Global Integrity, an organization that provides public and private sectors with expertise on fighting corruption, citizens can file complaints against the police with the Prosecutor General's Office, the Federal Security Service and the Department of Internal Security of the Ministry of the Interior. As well, in correspondence sent to the Research Directorate on 17 August 2011, the director of the Public Verdict Foundation states that the Department of Internal Security of the Ministry of the Interior and the Investigative Committee of the Russian Federation are the organizations that receive complaints against police officers. The Department of Internal Security of the Ministry of the Interior carries out investigations and decides the penalties to be imposed in cases of disciplinary infractions. The Investigative Committee of the Russian Federation, which was separated from the "procuracy" and is now an independent organization, processes complaints about crimes committed by police. According to Global Integrity, citizens can file complaints via the Internet site of the Ministry of the Interior by filling out a form online. As well, various NGOs, like the Public Verdict Foundation, help citizens file complaints against the police.
In June 2010, chief of the Department of Internal Security of the Ministry of the Interior stated that "'[i]n 2009 alone, over 27,000 citizens submitted complaints to internal security subunits' [of the Internal Security Department of the Ministry of the Interior]." In 2010, the number of complaints had increased by 50 percent. According to the chief, " '[i]n five months, 41,000 offenses committed by policemen have been exposed,'" which represents a 16 percent increase compared with the same period in 2009. The chief added that more than 2,000 crimes committed by policemen had been exposed. Global Integrity did not specify the nature of the complaints made against the police or of the crimes committed by them. The Paris daily newspaper Le Figaro reported on 22 February 2010 that, according to the president of Russia, 15,000 complaints of corruption were made against the police in 2009, which is only the " 'tip of the iceberg.'"23
[34] Also, documentary evidence before this panel indicates,
The Office of the Director of Religious Issues within the Office of the Federal Human Rights Ombudsman handles complaints dealing with religious freedom. The ombudsman can intercede on behalf of those who submit complaints, but cannot compel other government bodies to act.
The law entitles individuals and organizations to take religious freedom cases to the European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) in Strasbourg, which rules based on violations of the European Convention on Human Rights. According to the Constitutional Court, "Decisions by the European Court of Human Rights are binding for Russia. The State must pay compensation to a person whose rights were violated as determined by the European Court and ensure his/her rights are restored in as far as possible." The government complies with this ruling and continues to pay compensation in line with ECHR decisions.24
[35] The panel notes that there is no persuasive evidence before this panel that the claimant was persecuted and would be persecuted if she were to return. The panel prefers the documentary evidence over the claimant's testimony since they are drawn from a wide range of publicly accessible documents from reliable non-government and government organizations.
[36] The DR was asked if there are Jewish organizations in Russia that the claimant could approach for assistance with her complaints and he said that as far as he knew there were but he didn't know if one can complain there. Asked if she had complained to any and he replied that he didn't recall but he didn't think so. The panel notes that the Jewish population of Russia is estimated to be between 250,000 and 1,000,000 with 286 registered Jewish organizations to support them. Jewish leaders reported significant improvements in official attitudes towards Jews, but anti-Semitism remained a problem at the societal level. The SOVA Centre, an NGO that seeks to combat extremism and nationalism, monitored acts of anti-Semitism in 2010. From July to December 2010, the SOVA Center registered eight acts of anti-Semitic vandalism. There has been a reduction in vandalism due to a decrease in the activities of the nationalist groups Russian Way and Resistance, which had been very active in these crimes. According to the NGO Moscow Bureau for Human Rights, law enforcement officials are investigating vandalism in Voronezh, where 20 Jewish gravestones in a local Jewish cemetery were knocked down on July 27, 2010. On October 7, 2010 anti-Semitic inscriptions appeared on a Jewish synagogue in Barnaul and the local police are investigating that.25 This indicates that the state's effort over the years has dramatically improved the circumstances for Jews in Russia. Obviously not perfect, but clearly positive signs of progress.
[37] Violent attacks against Jews were infrequent, with only a few episodes occurring during 2011. The government has publicly criticized nationalist ideology and expressed support for legal action in response to anti-Semitic acts. While incidents still occurred and sometime authorities did not consider the racial motive, authorities continued to investigate and prosecute. On June 22, 2011, the central regional court of Khabarovsk convicted two individuals of committing arson at a local synagogue and attacking a police officer who had been investigating cases of extremism in 2009. The guilty parties received 24- and 27-month conditional sentences, respectively, and were released on probation.26 This indicates authorities do take action in crimes against Jews. It also indicates that authorities are sacrificing their own lives to stem extremism.
[38] Anti-Semitism on television or in other mainstream media was infrequent and was more likely to appear in low-circulation newspapers or in pamphlets. According to the Moscow Bureau of Human Rights (MBHR), anti-Semitic material on Russian-language Internet sites increased during the year. However, the state made serious efforts to combat this and there were several instances in which the government successfully prosecuted individuals for anti-Semitic statements or publications.27
[39] Sources indicate that there has been a revival of the Jewish religion and culture in Russia and that some Russian Jews who emigrated from Russia to Israel, USA, or other Western countries, are returning to Russia for job opportunities.28 Federal authorities, and in many cases regional and local authorities, facilitated the establishment of new Jewish institutions. The Federation of Jewish Communities reported that federal officials and some regional officials had been cooperative in the community's efforts to seek restitution of former synagogues, although some Jewish groups asserted that the government had returned only a small portion of the total properties confiscated during the Soviet period. A governmental pilot program in religious education that began in April 2010 continued in 19 regions, including the Muslim-majority North Caucasus and the Jewish Autonomous Region in the Far East.29
[40] Despite these efforts to combat extremism including anti-Semitism and the resultant improvements, it cannot be denied that anti-Semitism remains a serious societal problem. Also, while Russian government does not sanction anti-Semitism and there are laws aimed at punishing criminal acts involving nationality, race or religion more seriously, it is clear that authorities do not always investigate the racial motives for crimes properly which perpetuates the perception of impunity.30 However, according to SOVA, counteraction to anti-Semitism in Russia has substantially improved in Russia over the past few years, and promoting anti-Semitic propaganda was hardly prosecuted from 2004 to 2006, but from 2007 to 2008, 22 of 70 hate-speech convictions were for inciting anti-Semitism, although in 9 of the 22 cases, offenders received only probation sentences, and many high-profile ultra-nationalists who promote anti-Semitism were not prosecuted. The results of the efforts are apparent through to 2011 as noted above. (SOVA reports 30 acts of anti-Semitic vandalism in 2007 and 24 acts in 2008,31 and by 2010 there were only 8.
[41] The state is making serious efforts to combat anti-Semitism and extremism and there are positive signs of progress. Even if the claimant had experienced local failures or lack of professional decorum, the preponderance of evidence indicates that she could reasonably expect state protection would be forthcoming if she were to require it, particularly in the larger centres such as Moscow and St. Petersburg where the majority of Russia's Jews reside.32
[42] The claimant has not satisfied the panel with clear and convincing evidence that, on a balance of probabilities, the state would not be forthcoming with adequate protection. Changing societal attitudes is not an easy task, but there is no doubting the state's dedication and there is clear evidence of the state taking action against anti-Semitic and extremist criminal activity.
[43] The panel finds that the claimant was not a credible or trustworthy witness; therefore, it does not believe what the claimant alleges as her reasons for fearing returning to Russia. The panel finds that the claimant has not satisfied the burden of establishing a serious possibility of persecution for a Convention ground, or that she would personally be subjected, on a balance of probabilities, to a danger of torture, or a risk to life, or a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment upon return to her country. Therefore, the panel finds that the claimant is neither a Convention refugee nor a person in need of protection pursuant to sections 96 and 97(1) of the IRPA.
CONCLUSION
[44] After considering all the evidence before it, including counsel's submissions, the panel finds that the claimant is not a Convention refugee or a person in need of Canada's protection.
[45] The Division rejects her claim.
(signed)
"Edward C. Robinson"
Edward C. Robinson
February 19, 2014
Date
